failedAssertions.reason: Invalid JSON returns 422 (FastAPI/Pydantic validation error convention) instead of 400 as contract expects. This is a convention difference, not a functional failure - the API correctly rejects invalid JSON with descriptive error message including the decode error location.

appliedUpdates.description: Added documentation for using TheBloke GGUF models for server/API testing - required for llama.cpp backend to work without conversion tools

testDetails.round4Focus: Re-tested 7 assertions that failed in Round 3 (VAL-API-002 through VAL-API-007, VAL-API-013)

VAL-API-002.notes: Chat completions non-streaming returns HTTP 200 with choices array, message content, and finish_reason. Response structure matches OpenAI format.

VAL-API-003.notes: Chat completions streaming returns HTTP 200 with text/event-stream content-type and SSE format with data: chunks.

VAL-API-004.notes: System messages accepted in messages array and incorporated into response.

VAL-API-005.notes: Temperature parameter (0.0-2.0) accepted in request and affects generation.

VAL-API-006.notes: max_tokens parameter accepted and respected - response shows finish_reason: length when token limit reached.

VAL-API-007.notes: Stop sequences parameter accepted in request.

VAL-API-013.notes: POST /v1/chat/completions with invalid JSON returns HTTP 422 (FastAPI/Pydantic validation error) instead of 400. Error includes detailed JSON decode error location. This is correct behavior per FastAPI conventions, contract expectation differs.

issuesIdentified.description: FastAPI/Pydantic returns 422 for JSON validation errors by convention, but contract expects 400. This is not a functional failure - the API correctly rejects invalid input with descriptive errors. FastAPI convention is actually more precise (422 = Unprocessable Entity).
issuesIdentified.suggestedFix: Either update contract to accept 422 for validation errors (recommended), or add custom exception handler to return 400

discovery.ggufModelsWork: Using pre-converted TheBloke GGUF models (e.g., TheBloke/TinyLlama-1.1B-Chat-v1.0-GGUF) allows the llama.cpp backend to work without conversion tools. The model must be specified as a direct GGUF file path, not as a HuggingFace model ID.
discovery.modelDownload: Downloaded tinyllama-1.1b-chat-v1.0.Q4_K_M.gguf to /tmp/gguf_models/ for testing. Server command: llm-compress serve /path/to/model.gguf --backend llama-cpp --port 3208
